food_name: Coconut Milk, Thick (Full-Fat, Freshly Pressed or Canned)

ontology.foodex2_description: Coconut milk, full-fat (first pressing / thick), freshly pressed or canned
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for thick coconut milk as a food ingredient; SNOMED CT coverage for plant-based fat liquids is limited.

preparation_method: first pressing of grated coconut flesh

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of thick coconut milk used in cooking is approximately 100 ml (100 g), equivalent to the amount used to prepare one portion of nasi lemak rice or a single bowl of laksa broth.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut Milk, Thick — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F10060002; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy (NDB No. 12117)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values from HPB Singapore Foodo Database food code F10060002 (Coconut Milk, Thick). Cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 12117 (Coconuts, raw, coconut milk) for secondary micronutrient validation. Values are consistent between both sources within acceptable variance.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Coconut Milk — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 31 for coconut milk is consistent with values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database for coconut-based products. Coconut milk has very low carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g), resulting in a very low glycemic load. GI value of 31 (low) is supported by the low carbohydrate density and high fat content which attenuates glucose response. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (31 × 2.8) / 100 = 0.87, rounded to 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content (18.9 g/100 g), predominantly lauric acid (C12:0), which raises LDL cholesterol and is associated with increased cardiovascular risk and dyslipidaemia in patients with hypertension.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension and dyslipidaemia should significantly limit thick coconut milk consumption; substitute with reduced-fat or light coconut milk (approximately 10% fat), limit nasi lemak to once per week, and consider low-fat evaporated milk with coconut essence as a partial cooking substitute.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g) does not directly raise blood glucose; however, very high saturated fat (18.9 g/100 g) is associated with increased insulin resistance with regular consumption, and high caloric density (197 kcal/100 g) contributes to obesity risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thick coconut milk is acceptable in small quantities for people with type 2 diabetes given its low glycemic index (31) and low carbohydrate load, but portion size must be strictly controlled due to high saturated fat and caloric density, which can worsen insulin resistance and promote weight gain over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 18.9 g per 100 g — predominantly lauric acid (C12:0) — nearly meets the entire recommended daily saturated fat limit (20 g for a 2000 kcal diet) in a single 100 ml serving, raising LDL cholesterol and increasing atherosclerotic cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease or elevated LDL cholesterol should strictly limit thick coconut milk to occasional use only, preferring light coconut milk or non-coconut-based alternatives for regular cooking, as the lauric acid content significantly raises LDL despite also raising HDL, with an unfavourable net cardiovascular effect in non-traditional populations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat diets are associated with increased pro-inflammatory cytokines; however, lauric acid from coconut milk has shown some anti-inflammatory properties in laboratory studies, and the food provides useful magnesium (46 mg/100 g) and iron (3.3 mg/100 g).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thick coconut milk may be used in moderation by patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the anti-inflammatory potential of lauric acid is not sufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory risk of high saturated fat intake at large serving sizes, so portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thick coconut milk is the first pressing of freshly grated coconut flesh (Cocos nucifera) combined with a small amount of water, yielding a rich, opaque, creamy-white liquid with a dense, velvety consistency and a pronounced sweet coconut aroma. It is the essential fat and flavour base for Singapore and Southeast Asian iconic dishes including nasi lemak coconut rice, laksa broth, rendang, opor ayam, and most Peranakan curries, as well as South Indian fish and vegetable curries such as meen kuzhambu and avial. Commercially, Kara brand canned coconut milk is the standard reference product in Singapore. Nutritionally, thick coconut milk is extraordinarily high in saturated fat at 18.9 g per 100 g — predominantly lauric acid (C12:0) — meaning a single 100 ml serving nearly meets the entire recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20 g for a 2000 kcal diet. Despite this, it is cholesterol-free (0 mg), low in carbohydrates (2.8 g/100 g), and has a low glycemic index of 31, making it glycemically benign in small quantities. It provides meaningful amounts of potassium (263 mg), magnesium (46 mg), iron (3.3 mg), phosphorus (100 mg), and folate (16 mcg) per 100 g. The primary clinical concern is cardiovascular: regular consumption of coconut milk-heavy dishes significantly elevates LDL cholesterol and total saturated fat intake. Patients with hypertension, dyslipidaemia, or cardiovascular disease should use light coconut milk as a substitute. For type 2 diabetes, the low carbohydrate content is favourable but the high caloric density and saturated fat content require strict portion control to avoid worsening insulin resistance.
common_user_queries: thick coconut milk vs light coconut milk
how much saturated fat in coconut milk

coconut cream: very thick first pressing, higher fat than standard coconut milk

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database food code F10060002 (exact match), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 12117. Both approved primary sources are consistent. Ontology codes are defensible and verified. GI value of 31 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database data for coconut-based products.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for thick coconut milk not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo Database food code F10060002 or USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 12117 at the level of precision required; estimated water content is approximately 67-70 g/100 g based on composition (fat 21.3 g + protein 2.3 g + carbohydrate 2.8 g + fibre 2.2 g = 28.6 g solids, implying ~71 g water), but this is not confirmed by a direct source measurement and is therefore set to -1 pending verified data. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 12117 fatty acid profile for raw coconut milk; these values are approximate and may vary between freshly pressed and canned products. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo Database. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — coconut milk is a plant-derived product and naturally contains no cholesterol. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are reported as 0.0 per HPB Singapore Foodo Database F10060002; these are confirmed zero or below detection threshold for this food. GI value of 31 is supported by University of Sydney GI Database analogous evidence for coconut milk products; confidence is moderate as direct GI testing of thick coconut milk is limited in published literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (31 × 2.8) / 100 = 0.87, rounded to 0.9 per 100 g serving.
